summary: Updated tools/frank.py so its profiler-based instruction rescheduling operates only on the MWCC .text/code section rather than scanning and rewriting the entire object file. The script now extracts header, .text, and footer using the object code-size marker and a fixed MWCC code offset, runs rescheduling only on sliced code bytes, then reassembles the object. The PR also cleaned up repeated aligned-search logic and tightened safety checks around code-size mismatches and epilogue-local reorder handling.
what_changed: Added find_aligned(list, seq, idx), a helper that searches for byte sequences only at 4-byte-aligned positions and returns -1 when not found.
Introduced code_offset = 0x34 with a comment noting MWCC places the .text section at that file offset.
Factored code-size extraction into get_code_size(object_bytes), reading the 4-byte big-endian code size after CODESIZE_MAGIC.
Changed the no-code-object path to catch ValueError from get_code_size and write the vanilla object unchanged.
After reading vanilla and profile object bytes, the tool now slices out header, footer, vanilla .text bytes, and profile .text bytes, so rescheduling acts on code bytes only.
Tracked epilogue positions in epi_pos_list while scanning PROFILE_EXTRA_BYTES, enabling later epilogue-related reorder logic to inspect only known relevant locations.
Replaced repeated unaligned .find loops with find_aligned for PROFILE_EXTRA_BYTES, BLR byte sequences, MTLR, and BLR searches.
Added bounds checks around epilogue-local peephole rescheduling before reading surrounding instructions.
Changed final code construction from replacing a code region inside the full vanilla object to first producing final .text bytes, then reassembling header + final .text + footer.
Added a code-size mismatch guard: if stripped profile code length differs from vanilla .text length, the script warns and falls back to vanilla code because relocations may no longer line up.
Restricted the lmw/lwz/lfd reorder pass to epi_pos_list instead of scanning the entire final byte stream.
Kept the existing BLR restoration, mtlr/blr reunification, and lmw/lwz/lfd reorder concepts, but moved them into the .text-only processing flow.
smart_moves: The central fix was to isolate .text before performing byte-level scheduling edits, preventing accidental matches and rewrites in non-code regions of the object file.
Preserving header and footer bytes separately made the transformation explicit: only .text is mutable, while object metadata and non-code sections remain untouched.

The profile-code-size mismatch fallback is conservative and decomp-friendly: when stripped profile code cannot map one-to-one to vanilla code, it avoids producing relocated instructions at wrong offsets.
Using find_aligned consolidates a repeated safety pattern and reduces the chance that byte-pattern searches accidentally match instruction-like data at unaligned offsets.
Collecting epilogue positions while processing PROFILE_EXTRA_BYTES allowed the later lmw/lwz/lfd pass to be tied to known epilogue areas instead of scanning arbitrary bytes.
The change retained the no-code-object fast path, so objects without code continue to pass through unchanged.
decomp_lessons: Binary rewrite tools used for matching should clearly separate code sections from non-code sections before doing pattern replacement; object-wide byte scans are risky.
For MWCC object files in this context, the .text section can be treated as starting at offset 0x34, with size recoverable from the CODESIZE_MAGIC record.
When profiler-generated code has a different stripped length than vanilla code, do not blindly use it: relative relocation-sensitive instructions can become invalid if code offsets shift.
Pattern matching on PowerPC instruction streams should enforce 4-byte alignment.
Store contextual anchors discovered during one pass, such as epilogue positions, and reuse them to constrain later heuristic reorder passes.
When a tool is intended to help decomp matching, conservative fallback to vanilla bytes is preferable to producing an object that may silently corrupt non-code data or relocation-sensitive code.
naming_conventions: Helper names are descriptive and action-oriented, e.g. find_aligned and get_code_size.
The script uses explicit byte-region names: header, footer, vanilla_bytes, profile_bytes, final_bytes.
Code-size variables distinguish vanilla and profile values: vanilla_code_size and profile_code_size.
Epilogue-related state uses epi_pos and epi_pos_list.
Constants and magic byte arrays remain uppercase, e.g. CODESIZE_MAGIC, PROFILE_EXTRA_BYTES, BLR_BYTE_SEQ_ARRAY, MTLR_BYTE_SEQ, BLR_BYTE_SEQ.
Opcode mnemonic locals are uppercase, e.g. LWZ, LFS, ADDI, LI, LMW, FDIVS, LFD.
assembly_or_matching_tactics: Restrict byte-sequence searches and rewrites to the .text section instead of the whole object.
Use 4-byte-aligned searches for PowerPC instruction patterns.
Use CODESIZE_MAGIC plus a big-endian 4-byte size field to determine code length.
Strip PROFILE_EXTRA_BYTES from profile code before comparing/replacing final scheduled code.

For profile/vanilla schedule differences around inserted bl/nop profiler bytes, compare adjacent vanilla and profile instructions and use vanilla ordering when the profiler schedule swaps them undesirably.
Restore vanilla BLR-like branch-to-link-register instructions by copying matching aligned vanilla bytes back into final code.
Reunify separated mtlr/blr instructions by shifting intervening instructions up and moving mtlr closer to blr.
Apply the lmw/lwz/lfd reorder heuristic only at previously identified epilogue positions.
Assert final .text length matches expected code_size before reassembling the object.
follow_up_queries: Find later PRs that modify tools/frank.py after PR 956.
Search for CODESIZE_MAGIC usage in tooling to confirm object code-size parsing assumptions.
Look for other binary rewriting scripts that scan entire object files instead of section-limited code bytes.
Find PRs mentioning profile code size mismatch or relocation alignment problems.
Search for follow-up changes to lmw/lwz/lfd reorder heuristics or epi_pos_list.
